text: Topic: Performance vs Scalability. A service is scalable if it results in increased performance proportional to resources added. If you have a performance problem, your system is slow for a single user. If you have a scalability problem, your system is fast for a single user but slow under heavy load. Key insight: performance fixes optimize existing work; scalability fixes let you do more work in parallel.

Topic: Latency vs Throughput. Latency is the time to perform some action or produce some result. Throughput is the number of such actions or results per unit of time. Generally, you should aim for maximal throughput with acceptable latency. Example: a web server with 50ms latency and 1000 req/s throughput. Tradeoff: batching increases throughput but also increases latency for individual requests.

Topic: CAP Theorem (from System Design Primer). In a distributed system, you can only support two of three guarantees: Consistency (every read receives most recent write or error), Availability (every request gets a response, without guarantee of most recent data), Partition Tolerance (system works despite network partitions). Since networks aren't reliable, you must support partition tolerance — real choice is between CP and AP.

Topic: Consistency Patterns (from System Design Primer). Weak consistency: after a write, reads may or may not see it — best effort. Used in VoIP, video chat, real-time multiplayer games. Eventual consistency: after a write, reads will eventually see it (typically milliseconds). Used in DNS and email. Strong consistency: after a write, reads will see it immediately. Data replicated synchronously. Used in file systems and RDBMSes.

Topic: Availability Patterns (from System Design Primer). Active-passive failover: heartbeats between active and passive server. If heartbeat interrupted, passive takes over active's IP. Active-active: both servers manage traffic, spreading load. Disadvantages of failover: more hardware, complexity, potential data loss if active fails before replication. Availability in numbers: 99.9% = 8h 45min downtime/year; 99.99% = 52min downtime/year.

Topic: DNS (from System Design Primer). DNS translates domain names to IP addresses. It is hierarchical with authoritative servers at top level. Your router or ISP provides DNS server info. DNS results are cached by browser or OS determined by TTL. Record types: NS (name server), MX (mail exchange), A (name to IP), CNAME (name to another name). DNS services can route traffic via weighted round robin, latency-based, or geolocation-based methods.

Topic: CDN (from System Design Primer). A CDN is a globally distributed network of proxy servers serving content from locations closer to users. Push CDN: you upload content directly, minimizing traffic but maximizing storage. Good for sites with small traffic or infrequently updated content. Pull CDN: grabs content from your server on first request, caches with TTL. Good for heavy-traffic sites. Disadvantages: cost, stale content before TTL expires, URL changes needed.

Topic: Load Balancer (from System Design Primer). Load balancers distribute incoming requests to computing resources. Benefits: prevent unhealthy server requests, prevent overloading, eliminate single points of failure. Additional benefits: SSL termination (decrypt incoming, encrypt responses), session persistence via cookies. Can route based on: random, least loaded, session/cookies, round robin, Layer 4 (transport), or Layer 7 (application).

Topic: Layer 4 vs Layer 7 Load Balancing (from System Design Primer). Layer 4 load balancers look at transport layer info (source/dest IP, ports) to distribute requests. They forward network packets using NAT. Layer 7 load balancers look at application layer (headers, message, cookies). They terminate connections, read messages, make routing decisions, then open connections to selected servers. Layer 7 enables content-based routing (e.g., video traffic to video servers).

Topic: Reverse Proxy (from System Design Primer). A reverse proxy is a web server that centralizes internal services and provides unified interfaces to the public. Benefits: increased security (hide backend server info), scalability and flexibility (add/remove backend servers freely), SSL termination, compression, caching, and serving static content directly. Difference from load balancer: useful even with just one web server.

Topic: Microservices (from System Design Primer). A suite of independently deployable, small, modular services. Each service runs a unique process and communicates through a well-defined lightweight mechanism. Pinterest example: separate services for user profile, follower, feed, search, photo upload. Service discovery: tools like Consul, Etcd, or Zookeeper help services find each other. Tradeoff: adds complexity of distributed systems.

Topic: Database Replication (from System Design Primer). Master-slave replication: master serves reads and writes, replicates to one or more slaves which serve only reads. If master goes offline, system operates in read-only until a slave is promoted. Master-master replication: both masters serve reads and writes, coordinate on writes. If either goes down, the other continues. Disadvantages: additional logic needed, loosely consistent, increased latency from synchronization.

Topic: Database Federation (from System Design Primer). Federation (functional partitioning) splits databases by function. For example: separate databases for users, products, and forums. Smaller databases result in more data fitting in memory, leading to more cache hits and faster reads/writes. Disadvantages: not effective if schema requires huge functions/tables, adds logic to determine which database to read/write, joining data from two databases is complex.

Topic: Database Sharding (from System Design Primer). Sharding distributes data across databases such that each database manages only a subset of the data. Benefits: less read/write traffic per shard, less replication, more cache hits, smaller index = faster queries. Common approaches: hash-based (user_id mod N), range-based, directory-based. Disadvantages: complex application logic, uneven data distribution (hotspots), joining across shards is complex, rebalancing adds complexity.

Topic: Cache Update Strategies (from System Design Primer). Cache-aside: app looks in cache first, on miss reads from DB and populates cache. Write-through: app writes to cache, cache synchronously writes to DB. Slow for writes but reads are fast. Write-behind (write-back): app writes to cache, cache asynchronously writes to DB. Improved write performance. Refresh-ahead: automatically refresh recently accessed entries before expiration. Reduces latency but can waste resources on inaccurate predictions.

Topic: Asynchronism (from System Design Primer). Message queues receive, hold, and deliver messages. If an operation is too slow to perform inline, use a message queue with workflow: user not blocked, job processed in background, user notified when complete. Examples: Redis as message broker, RabbitMQ, Amazon SQS. Task queues receive tasks and their data, run them, then deliver results. Back pressure: limit queue size to maintain high throughput and good response times for queued items.

Topic: TCP (from System Design Primer). TCP is a connection-oriented protocol over an IP network. Connection established and terminated using a handshake. All packets sent are guaranteed to reach the destination in the original order and without corruption — through sequence numbers, checksum fields, and acknowledgments. If sender doesn't receive a correct response, it resends. TCP also implements flow control and congestion control. Higher overhead than UDP, good for reliability.

Topic: UDP (from System Design Primer). UDP is connectionless — datagrams (analogous to packets) are guaranteed only at the datagram level. Datagrams might arrive out of order or not at all. UDP doesn't support congestion control. Without the guarantees that TCP provides, UDP is generally more efficient. UDP can broadcast datagrams to all devices on the subnet. Useful with DHCP because the client hasn't received an IP yet. Less reliable but less latency.

Topic: REST vs RPC (from System Design Primer). REST is an architectural approach where client actions (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) are on resources — focused on exposing data. REST uses standard HTTP, good for public APIs. RPC focuses on exposing behaviors — clients invoke procedures on the server. RPC can be more performant with custom binary protocols (protobuf). Good for internal microservice communication where performance matters more than standardization.

Topic: Back-of-the-Envelope Calculations (from System Design Primer). Essential for system design interviews. Key numbers: 2.5 million seconds per month. 1 req/s ≈ 2.5M req/month. 40 req/s ≈ 100M req/month. 400 req/s ≈ 1B req/month. Powers of two: 10 = 1KB, 20 = 1MB, 30 = 1GB, 40 = 1TB. Latency: L1 cache ref 0.5ns, branch mispredict 5ns, RAM ref 100ns, SSD read 150μs, HDD seek 10ms, round-trip within same datacenter 500μs.

Topic: Design Pastebin/URL Shortener (from System Design Primer). Use cases: user enters text, gets random link; user enters URL, views contents. Constraints: 10M users, 10M writes/month, 100M reads/month (10:1 read/write ratio). Core design: Client → Web Server (reverse proxy) → Write API → generates unique URL, stores in SQL + Object Store (S3). Read path: check cache (Redis/Memcached) → SQL for shortlink → Object Store for content. Scale with read replicas, CDN, separate read/write APIs.

Topic: Availability in Numbers (from System Design Primer). 99.9% (three 9s): 8h 45min downtime/year, 43min/month, 10min/week. 99.99% (four 9s): 52min/year, 4min/month, 1min/week, 8.6s/day. Availability in sequence: Avail(Total) = Avail(Foo) × Avail(Bar). Two services at 99.9% in sequence = 99.8%. Availability in parallel: Avail(Total) = 1 - (1-Avail(Foo)) × (1-Avail(Bar)). Two services at 99.9% in parallel = 99.9999%.
